# Management of High-Risk and Special Populations

## Double-Hit Lymphoma: DA-EPOCH-R Versus R-CHOP

High-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL2 and/or BCL6 rearrangements (double-hit lymphoma, DHL) represents one of the most challenging frontline treatment dilemmas. Patients with DHL experience significantly inferior outcomes with standard R-CHOP, with 5-year time-to-progression rates of approximately 57% for DHITsig-positive patients compared with 81% for DHITsig-negative patients (P < .001) [@Ennishi2019Doublehit]. The molecular high-grade (MHG) group, comprising approximately 9% of DLBCL cases and overlapping substantially with DHL, carries a particularly poor prognosis that is independent of the International Prognostic Index [@Sha2019Molecular].

Dose-adjusted EPOCH-R (DA-EPOCH-R) has been widely adopted as the preferred regimen for DHL based on retrospective and single-arm prospective data, despite the absence of randomized evidence demonstrating superiority over R-CHOP in this specific population. The Alliance/CALGB 50303 trial, which compared DA-EPOCH-R with R-CHOP in unselected DLBCL (n = 491), showed no PFS advantage for the intensive regimen (HR, 0.93; 95% CI, 0.68--1.27) but was not powered to detect differences in the DHL subgroup [@Bartlett2019Doseadjusted]. Retrospective series have suggested improved outcomes with DA-EPOCH-R in DHL, with complete response rates of 68--80% and 2-year PFS of approximately 60--65%, but selection bias and small sample sizes limit the strength of these conclusions. The POLARIX trial included patients with DHL, and subgroup analyses suggested that pola-R-CHP may offer benefit in this population, though dedicated prospective studies are needed [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab]. More recently, frontline CAR-T therapy has been explored in high-risk DHL/THL as part of the ZUMA-12 trial, with encouraging early results showing a 78% complete response rate in the overall high-risk population [@Neelapu2022Axicabtagene].

## CNS Prophylaxis

Central nervous system (CNS) relapse occurs in 2--5% of DLBCL patients overall but reaches 10% or higher in high-risk subgroups, with devastating consequences including a median survival of only 2--6 months after CNS relapse [@Schmitz2016CNS]. The CNS-IPI risk model, developed from a cohort of 2,164 patients treated with R-CHOP, stratifies patients into three risk categories based on the five standard IPI factors plus kidney and/or adrenal gland involvement [@Schmitz2016CNS]. Two-year CNS disease rates are 0.6% for low-risk (46% of patients), 3.4% for intermediate-risk (41%), and 10.2% for high-risk (12%) patients [@Schmitz2016CNS].

The optimal approach to CNS prophylaxis remains contentious. Intrathecal methotrexate (IT-MTX), typically administered with each R-CHOP cycle, is the most widely used strategy due to its simplicity and low systemic toxicity. However, IT-MTX achieves limited penetration into brain parenchyma, and retrospective data have questioned its efficacy in preventing parenchymal relapse. High-dose systemic methotrexate (HD-MTX, typically 3--3.5 g/m^2^), which achieves therapeutic CNS concentrations, has emerged as an alternative, often administered as an intercalation between R-CHOP cycles or as consolidation after completing chemoimmunotherapy [@Grommes2019Comprehensive]. The primary challenge with HD-MTX is its significant toxicity profile, including nephrotoxicity and mucositis, which may compromise delivery of the primary chemotherapy regimen. No randomized trial has definitively compared IT-MTX with HD-MTX for CNS prophylaxis in DLBCL, and practice patterns vary considerably across institutions. Current guidelines recommend CNS prophylaxis for patients with CNS-IPI high-risk features and those with specific high-risk extranodal sites (testis, breast, adrenal, kidney) or double-hit biology [@SusanibarAdaniya2021].

## Elderly and Unfit Patients

DLBCL is predominantly a disease of older adults, with a median age at diagnosis of approximately 65 years. Elderly patients (typically defined as age 80 years or older) and those with significant comorbidities frequently cannot tolerate full-dose R-CHOP due to increased risks of treatment-related morbidity and mortality, particularly cardiac toxicity from doxorubicin and infectious complications from myelosuppression [@Coiffier2016Diffuse].

R-miniCHOP, a dose-attenuated regimen reducing doxorubicin to 25 mg/m^2^, cyclophosphamide to 400 mg/m^2^, and vincristine to 1 mg, with standard-dose rituximab and prednisone, has become a widely adopted approach for patients older than 80 years. Seminal studies demonstrated median overall survival exceeding 2 years with R-miniCHOP in this population, substantially outperforming best supportive care.

The POLARIX trial enrolled patients aged 18--80 years, and the favorable toxicity profile of pola-R-CHP (with elimination of vincristine-associated neuropathy) may offer particular advantages in older patients [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab]. Post-hoc analyses have suggested that pola-R-CHP may be especially beneficial in patients aged 60 years or older, where the peripheral neuropathy of vincristine is most problematic. For patients truly unfit for anthracycline-based chemotherapy, alternative approaches including bendamustine-rituximab, lenalidomide-rituximab, or single-agent therapies remain options, though cure rates are significantly lower [@Flinn2019Firstline; @Ghesquieres2019Lenalidomide].

## Young Fit Patients and Intensive Approaches

Young, fit patients (typically age 18--60 years) with high-risk DLBCL (age-adjusted IPI 2--3) represent a population where intensification strategies have been extensively explored. The DLCL04 phase III trial evaluated rituximab-dose-dense chemotherapy with or without consolidative high-dose chemotherapy plus autologous stem cell transplantation in patients aged 18--65 years with high-risk disease [@Chiappella2017Rituximabdosedense]. The transplantation arm did not demonstrate a survival advantage, suggesting that upfront ASCT consolidation does not improve outcomes in the rituximab era for unselected high-risk patients.

The age-dependent benefit of ibrutinib plus R-CHOP observed in the Phoenix trial offers a more targeted intensification strategy for younger patients with non-GCB DLBCL. In patients aged 60 years or younger, ibrutinib plus R-CHOP significantly improved EFS (HR, 0.579), PFS (HR, 0.556), and OS (HR, 0.330), with manageable toxicity [@Younes2019Randomized]. Genetic subtype analysis revealed that this benefit was concentrated in the MCD and N1 molecular subtypes, with 3-year EFS of 100% for ibrutinib-R-CHOP compared with 42.9--50% for R-CHOP alone [@Wilson2021Effect]. Frontline CAR-T therapy for high-risk young patients is under active investigation, with the ZUMA-12 and ZUMA-23 trials exploring this approach [@Neelapu2022Axicabtagene].

## Interim PET-Adapted Therapy

Interim positron emission tomography (iPET) after two to four cycles of R-CHOP has emerged as a powerful prognostic tool and potential guide for treatment adaptation. Patients achieving a complete metabolic response (Deauville score 1--3) on iPET have excellent outcomes with continued standard therapy, while those with persistent PET positivity (Deauville score 4--5) face substantially inferior prognosis, with 2-year PFS rates of only 30--40% [@Lewis2020Lymphoma].

PET-adapted strategies aim to intensify treatment for iPET-positive patients (escalation) or de-intensify for iPET-negative patients (de-escalation). The FLYER trial demonstrated that reduced chemotherapy is appropriate for favorable-risk limited-stage patients, and iPET may further refine the identification of patients eligible for de-escalation [@Poeschel2019Four]. For iPET-positive patients, switching to salvage regimens, high-dose therapy with ASCT, or novel agents is under investigation in several ongoing trials. However, the optimal response to a positive iPET remains uncertain, as false-positive results can occur due to inflammation and reactive processes, and the predictive value of iPET varies by Deauville score threshold and timing of assessment.

The integration of iPET with circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) monitoring represents an evolving strategy. Liquid biopsy approaches have shown that rapid clearance of tumor mutations from cell-free DNA during R-CHOP correlates with treatment response, while persistent ctDNA detection identifies patients at high risk for treatment failure [@Rossi2017Diffuse]. The combination of metabolic imaging and molecular monitoring may provide complementary prognostic information, enabling more precise risk-adapted treatment decisions in the frontline setting. The five-year follow-up data from ZUMA-1 demonstrated that a substantial proportion of patients with R/R LBCL treated with axi-cel achieved durable responses, supporting the curative potential of CAR-T therapy and the rationale for its investigation in high-risk patients who demonstrate early treatment failure on iPET assessment [@Neelapu2023Fiveyear].
